CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion) and the main contributions are stated early (intro outlines the PL‑CNN class and the CCCP+BCFW layerwise algorithm). Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. PL functions (Eq.1), layer parameters W^l, representation Φ, Propositions 1–3 and Algorithm 1), which helps follow the technical development. Presentation issues are minor: the provided text contains OCR/duplication glitches and missing figure/table displays (references to Table 1, Figures 1–3 and some duplicated paragraphs in Section E) that interrupt reading and slightly reduce clarity, but the exposition and arguments remain largely clear and accessible. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion), and the main contributions (PL‑CNN formulation, CCCP+BCFW layerwise algorithm and BCFW extensions) are stated clearly early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. W^l, Φ(x;W), objective (3)) and the method section systematically presents propositions with proof sketches, which aids understanding. Presentation suffers from some formatting/OCR issues and minor clarity problems (e.g. awkwardly typeset symbols like "W svmy", repeated/duplicated paragraphs in Appendix E, and many figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text), but these are presentation issues rather than structural ones and do not prevent following the main ideas.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion) and the main contributions are stated early (intro outlines the PL‑CNN class and the CCCP+BCFW layerwise algorithm). Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. PL functions (Eq.1), layer parameters W^l, representation Φ, Propositions 1–3 and Algorithm 1), which helps follow the technical development. Presentation issues are minor: the provided text contains OCR/duplication glitches and missing figure/table displays (references to Table 1, Figures 1–3 and some duplicated paragraphs in Section E) that interrupt reading and slightly reduce clarity, but the exposition and arguments remain largely clear and accessible.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Introduction → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion), and the main contributions (PL‑CNN formulation, CCCP+BCFW layerwise algorithm and BCFW extensions) are stated clearly early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and defined (e.g. W^l, Φ(x;W), objective (3)) and the method section systematically presents propositions with proof sketches, which aids understanding. Presentation suffers from some formatting/OCR issues and minor clarity problems (e.g. awkwardly typeset symbols like "W svmy", repeated/duplicated paragraphs in Appendix E, and many figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text), but these are presentation issues rather than structural ones and do not prevent following the main ideas.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The core technical claims are clearly stated and largely supported: Propositions 1–5 (composition of PL functions, DC reformulation of a layer, forward/backward oracle, proximal initialization, and memory reduction) are plausible and accompanied by derivations (with the detailed dual/BCFW derivation in Appendix D) that are internally consistent. The CCCP+BCFW methodology and the analytic step-size in the dual are correctly derived. Concerns are minor and practical: several proofs are only sketched rather than fully formalized, the crucial speed/memory/runtime trade-offs and convergence behaviour (especially when approximating the latent search by restricting to Y) lack theoretical guarantees, and experiments report point improvements without statistical variance or detailed runtime baselines; also the method requires fixing batch-norm statistics (acknowledged by the authors). These do not invalidate the main findings but leave some empirical and theoretical gaps. | B(4): The paper’s central technical claims are well-formulated and supported: Proposition 1 (closure of PL under composition), Proposition 2 (layer-wise objective as a DC / latent SVM), and Proposition 3 (activation argmax via forward pass and feature via backward pass) are mathematically plausible and consistent with the provided derivations/appendices. The BCFW dual derivation, the proximal (trust-region) initialization and the memory-saving chain-rule representation (Prop.5) are sound and correctly derived. Remaining issues are minor: many proofs are sketched rather than fully presented in the main text (though appendices are referenced), the empirical strategy fine-tunes from already-trained SGD solutions (which may bias comparisons), experiments report point results without statistical variance or multiple runs, and the method requires fixing BN statistics (limiting applicability). These do not invalidate the technical contributions but warrant caution in generality and empirical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The core technical claims are clearly stated and largely supported: Propositions 1–5 (composition of PL functions, DC reformulation of a layer, forward/backward oracle, proximal initialization, and memory reduction) are plausible and accompanied by derivations (with the detailed dual/BCFW derivation in Appendix D) that are internally consistent. The CCCP+BCFW methodology and the analytic step-size in the dual are correctly derived. Concerns are minor and practical: several proofs are only sketched rather than fully formalized, the crucial speed/memory/runtime trade-offs and convergence behaviour (especially when approximating the latent search by restricting to Y) lack theoretical guarantees, and experiments report point improvements without statistical variance or detailed runtime baselines; also the method requires fixing batch-norm statistics (acknowledged by the authors). These do not invalidate the main findings but leave some empirical and theoretical gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central technical claims are well-formulated and supported: Proposition 1 (closure of PL under composition), Proposition 2 (layer-wise objective as a DC / latent SVM), and Proposition 3 (activation argmax via forward pass and feature via backward pass) are mathematically plausible and consistent with the provided derivations/appendices. The BCFW dual derivation, the proximal (trust-region) initialization and the memory-saving chain-rule representation (Prop.5) are sound and correctly derived. Remaining issues are minor: many proofs are sketched rather than fully presented in the main text (though appendices are referenced), the empirical strategy fine-tunes from already-trained SGD solutions (which may bias comparisons), experiments report point results without statistical variance or multiple runs, and the method requires fixing BN statistics (limiting applicability). These do not invalidate the technical contributions but warrant caution in generality and empirical claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable and non-obvious perspective by showing that layerwise parameter estimation for PL-CNNs can be written as a difference-of-convex program / latent structured SVM (Proposition 1 & 2) and thus optimized via CCCP + structured-SVM solvers. While it builds on existing tools (CCCP, BCFW), the work non-trivially adapts them to deep nets (trust-region initialization, memory-efficient feature representation, constraint-reduction) and claims practical benefits (monotonic objective decrease, no tuned learning rate). This is a clear, differentiated contribution (new formulation + algorithmic extensions) that would inform experts, though it leverages known optimization primitives rather than inventing wholly new algorithms. | B(4): The paper presents a genuinely new perspective by showing that layer-wise parameter estimation for PL-CNNs can be formulated as a latent structured SVM / DC program (Sec. 4.1, Proposition 2), which appears to be a hitherto-unknown connection. It then leverages established optimization tools (CCCP and BCFW, Sec. 4.2–4.3) and provides non-trivial, practical extensions (trust-region initialization, memory-efficient feature representation, constraint reduction) tailored to deep networks. Thus the work is more than a simple combination of known pieces: it offers a notable, well-differentiated viewpoint and technical adaptations that would inform experts, though it builds on existing optimization primitives rather than introducing wholly new algorithms.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a notable and non-obvious perspective by showing that layerwise parameter estimation for PL-CNNs can be written as a difference-of-convex program / latent structured SVM (Proposition 1 & 2) and thus optimized via CCCP + structured-SVM solvers. While it builds on existing tools (CCCP, BCFW), the work non-trivially adapts them to deep nets (trust-region initialization, memory-efficient feature representation, constraint-reduction) and claims practical benefits (monotonic objective decrease, no tuned learning rate). This is a clear, differentiated contribution (new formulation + algorithmic extensions) that would inform experts, though it leverages known optimization primitives rather than inventing wholly new algorithms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a genuinely new perspective by showing that layer-wise parameter estimation for PL-CNNs can be formulated as a latent structured SVM / DC program (Sec. 4.1, Proposition 2), which appears to be a hitherto-unknown connection. It then leverages established optimization tools (CCCP and BCFW, Sec. 4.2–4.3) and provides non-trivial, practical extensions (trust-region initialization, memory-efficient feature representation, constraint reduction) tailored to deep networks. Thus the work is more than a simple combination of known pieces: it offers a notable, well-differentiated viewpoint and technical adaptations that would inform experts, though it builds on existing optimization primitives rather than introducing wholly new algorithms.
